coredata.dc:title: On-site quantitation of xanthine in fish and serum using a smartphone-based spectrophotometer integrated with a dual-readout nanosensing assay
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ningxia
Xiao, Meng
Yu, Zipei
Jin, Baohui
Yang, Mengsu
Yi, Changqing
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and quantitative biochemical analysis at points-of-need is imperative for food safety inspection. This work reports on: 1) a stand-alone smartphone-based “two-in-one” spectrophotometer (the SAFS) installed with a self-developed application (the SAFS-App) which can precisely collect both absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra in a reproducible manner within 5 s; and 2) a straightforward protocol for xanthine detection using fluorescent carbon nanodots and silver nanoparticles. The assay performed with the SAFS demonstrates high specificity towards xanthine, and a linear range of 1–60 μM with LODs of 0.38 and 0.58 μM for colorimetric and fluorometric readouts, respectively. The reliability and robustness of the SAFS are validated by on-site quantitation of xanthine in fish and serum samples, with comparable accuracy to HPLC method. More importantly, the SAFS presents itself as an appealing device which is accessible to everyone through the Internet of Things and can be tailored for diverse point-of-care testing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xanthine
Carbon nanodots
Silver nanoparticle
Inner filter effect
Portable device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the SAFS and development of the SAFS-App
Colorimetric and fluorometric detection of xanthine
Fabrication of the SAFS and development of the SAFS-App
Synthesis and characterization of CDs and AgNPs
Colorimetric and fluorometric detection of xanthine
On-site detection of xanthine in serum and fish using the SAFS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of design of a smartphone-based “two-in-one” spectrophotometer (the SAFS) installed with a custom-designed App (the SAFS-App) for collecting both absorption and fluorescence spectra, and rational of on-site quantitation of xanthine with colorimetric and fluorometric dual-readouts using CDs and AgNPs.

Schematic illustration of the SAFS. (a) Parts assembly drawing of the SAFS; (b) Photograph of the actual SAFS installed on a Note 6 smartphone. (c-f) Internal optical paths (c, e) and photographs (d, f) of the SAFS for collecting absorption spectra (c, d) and fluorescence spectra (e, f). Insets show the key optical elements which are assembled into the cradle in a plug-and-play manner. (g) Flowchart showing the operation processing steps of the SAFS-APP.

Spectrum collection consistency of Fluoromax-4P fluorescence spectrophotometer (a), the SAFS (b) and DU730 UV/vis spectrophotometer (c). Fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs, coumarin 6, rhodamine 6G and the mixture rhodamine 6G/CDs collected by Fluoromax-4P fluorescence spectrophotometer (a) and the SAFS with the fluorometric mode (the upper row of (b)). Absorption spectrum of trypan blue, the AgNPs, rhodamine 6G and the mixture of trypan blue/AgNPs collected by the SAFS with the colorimetric mode (the lower row of (b)) and DU730 UV/vis spectrophotometer (c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a-b) TEM images of the as-prepared CDs (a) and AgNPs (b). Insets at left lower corner of (a) and (b) showed size distribution of the CDs and the AgNPs; Insets at upper right corner of (a) showed HRTEM image of the CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs (curve i) and the AgNPs (curve ii), fluorescence excitation spectrum (curve iii) and emission spectrum (curve iv) of the CDs; (d-g) Absorption spectra (d) and fluorescence spectra (λex = 365 nm) (f) of the CDs/AgNPs indicator solution upon addition of 1.0 mg/mL xanthine oxidase and 0–100 μM xanthine. Linear plots of absorbance decrease (e) and fluorescence restoration (g) of the CDs/AgNPs indicator solution as a function of the xanthine concentration. (h-i) The selectivity of the dual-readout xanthine sensing assay to possible coexisting substance in the absence and presence of xanthine. (h) colorimetric readout; (i) fluorometric readout.

(a) Sensitivity of performing the dual-readout xanthine sensing assay with the SAFS. Insets show the corresponding images of the CDs/AgNPs indicator solution upon reaction with detection solution containing xanthine oxidase and standard xanthine solutions of different concentrations (0, 1, 10, 20, 40, 50, 60 µM) captured by the SAFS; (b) The detection consistency of the SAFS and laboratory desktop spectrophotometer; (c) The xanthine concentration change in fish versus its storage time; (d) Bland-Altman analysis of quantitation results of xanthine in fish homogenate by HPLC and the SAFS. (i) Colorimetric and (ii) fluorometric readout.
The determination of Xanthine in spiked fish and serum samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: aResults are expressed as the mean ± S.D. (n = 3). b Recovery (%) = (detected concentration/spiked concentration) × 100. ND: not detectable.

head.ce:title: On-site quantitation of xanthine in fish and serum using a smartphone-based spectrophotometer integrated with a dual-readout nanosensing assay

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Ningxia Xu, Meng Xiao, and Zipei Yu contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and quantitative biochemical analysis at points-of-need is imperative for food safety inspection. This work reports on: 1) a stand-alone smartphone-based “two-in-one” spectrophotometer (the SAFS) installed with a self-developed application (the SAFS-App) which can precisely collect both absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra in a reproducible manner within 5 s; and 2) a straightforward protocol for xanthine detection using fluorescent carbon nanodots and silver nanoparticles. The assay performed with the SAFS demonstrates high specificity towards xanthine, and a linear range of 1–60 μM with LODs of 0.38 and 0.58 μM for colorimetric and fluorometric readouts, respectively. The reliability and robustness of the SAFS are validated by on-site quantitation of xanthine in fish and serum samples, with comparable accuracy to HPLC method. More importantly, the SAFS presents itself as an appealing device which is accessible to everyone through the Internet of Things and can be tailored for diverse point-of-care testing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Optical detection has been widely explored in food safety inspection (Xu et al., 2020), biochemical analysis (Kolanowski, Liu, & New, 2018), clinical diagnostics (Yurdakul et al., 2020), and environmental monitoring (Long, Jiang, Shangguan, Qing, Zhang, & Feng, 2021). Current optical detection tremendously depends on laboratory-based sophisticated instruments and professional operators, which substantially restricts their applications in point-of-care testing (POCT). The dramatically increasing demands for optical detection in a field-deployable manner motivate the development of portable spectrophotometers which can precisely collect spectra and perform accurate quantitation. However, there is still a lack of cost-effective and easy-to-use portable spectrophotometers for optical detection at point-of-need by laypersons.

Notably, it has been well-established that combination of multiple types of reporting signals can reduce the impact of background interference and systematic errors, thus realizing more accurate detection. Typical examples include determination of pH (L. Chen et al., 2021a; Fang et al., 2020), Fe3+ (Cong et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2021), histone demethylases (Deng et al., 2020), acetylcholinesterase activity (Zhao, Chen, Sun, & Yang, 2016), glutathione (Shi et al., 2014; Jin Wang, Liu, Li, Chen, & Xu, 2021) and H2O2 (X. Huang et al., 2018) using colorimetric and fluorometric signals, quantitation of platelet-derived growth factor-BB using electrochemical and colorimetric signals (Y. Huang et al., 2021), the immunosensor with electrochemiluminescent, electrochemical and fluorometric triple-readouts (Qian, Zhang, Cao, & Liu, 2010). Particularly, the combination of colorimetric and fluorometric signals is the most extensively explored for analytical and biomedical detections, because colorimetric detection allows direct visual analysis while fluorometric detection provides high sensitivity (Shi et al., 2014; Jin Wang et al., 2021).Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a portable spectrophotometer that can precisely collect both absorption and fluorometric spectra for realizing point-of-need quantitation, especially in resource-limited regions.

Very recently, smartphone is becoming a promising platform to address the critical issues of portable instrument development such as accessibility, applicability and user-friendliness, thanks to its “smart”, ubiquity, the open-source operation system, data-processing capability, and connectivity (Liu, Xiao, Xu, Yang, & Yi, 2022; Xiao et al., 2022). Through being integrated with specifically designed peripheral attachments and custom-designed applications (App), smartphones have been successfully conditioned as portable spectrophotometers (W. Chen, Yao, Chen, Shen, Tang, & Lee, 2021b), electrochemical workstation (Cheng, Gong, Yang, Zheng, Zheng, Li, Xu, Nie, Xie, Chen, Yi, & Jiang, 2022; Z. Xu, Liu, Xiao, Jiang, & Yi, 2020), microscope (Chung et al., 2021). In particular, because of high-resolution cameras together with appropriate optical elements, smartphone-based portable spectrophotometers are extensively explored to precisely collect various types of spectra, such as adsorption spectra (Jian et al., 2019; Kong et al., 2020), fluorescence spectra (Yu, Tan, & Cunningham, 2014), surface enhanced Raman scattering spectra (SERS) (Zeng et al., 2019), and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) (Jinling Zhang et al., 2018). Despite urgently needed, it is still challenging to develop smartphone-based portable spectrophotometers that can simultaneously collect multiple types of spectra.

Xanthine, 3,7-dihydropurine-2,6-dione, is an intermediate in the metabolic process of purine nucleotides. As part of the purine degradation pathway, xanthine undergoes enzymatic breakdown by xanthine oxidase, resulting in the production of uric acid and H2O2. As a result, the potential of xanthine as an indicator for abnormal purine profile-related pathological conditions has been extensively reported (Z. Li et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2020). In addition, xanthine can also serve as an indicator of freshness of fish meat, because adenosine triphosphate (ATP) rapidly degrades into xanthine in fish tissues after theirdeath (Dervisevic et al., 2015; C. Li, Hao, & Wu, 2019). Monitoring these ATP degradation changes provides an accurate means of evaluating meat freshness, where xanthine can serve as an inchoate indicator long before any visible spoilages (Gu et al., 2023). Even when any direct putrefaction toxicology is yet to present, excessive xanthine can elevate uric acid levels, subsequently causing gout, hyperuricemia, and subsequently diabetes, cerebral ischemia, nonalcoholic fatty liver disease as well as cardiovascular diseases (Shih, Lazo, Liu, Bonekamp, Hernaez, & Clark, 2015). Therefore, quantitation of xanthine plays an important role in not only biomedical diagnosis but also freshness evaluation of meat.

In this study, we successfully develop a smartphone-based “two-in-one” spectrophotometer (referred to as the SAFS) that can precisely collect the absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra in the wavelength range of 380–800 nm. A Java language-based App, the SAFS-App, is programmed to directly convert both absorbance and fluorescence changes at each wavelength of the whole visible region to digital data, plot the spectra, establish calibration curves, display results and testing locations, generate and share reports (Fig. 1 ). In addition, a new dual-readout nanosensing assay for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of xanthine using fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) is developed. On-site quantitation of xanthine in fish and serum samples is successfully realized by performing the reported dual-readout nanosensing assay with the stand-alone SAFS, validating its capability and practicability.

Noteworthy, this study highlights the following distinctive advantages: 1) an absorption and fluorescence “two-in-one” portable spectrophotometer featured with cost-effectiveness and ease-of-use is successfully developed by taking advantage of smartphones. This provides a powerful sensing device accessible to everyone through the Internet of Things applications, and a versatile optical detection platform for further developing diverse POCT applications; 2) a straightforward dual-readout protocol for rapid detection of xanthine is established by employing xanthine oxidase as the recognition moiety while AgNPs and CDs as the colorimetric and fluorometric indicator, respectively. This provides an accurate sensing scheme that can be tailored to quantitate other analytes by simply changing the recognition moiety; 3) point-of-need quantitation of xanthine can be realized by laypersons in a reproducible manner, thanks to the ease-of-use SAFS, the user-friendly SAFS-App and the straightforward protocol. This provides a scalable and portable solution for food safety inspection, home-health monitoring, and environment surveillance, especially in low-resource settings.

Trisodium citrate (99.8%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, crystal), citric acid (99.5%) and urea was purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride and potassium chloride was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagents (Guangzhou, China). l-ascorbic acid, glutathione, glycine, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 3%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Xanthine oxidase, xanthine, uanine and uric acid was purchased from Meilunbio Technology Co., Ltd (Dalian, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q system and used for all the experiments.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded by a DU730 UV/vis spectrophotometer (Beckman, Germany) and a Fluoromax-4P fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained with a ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100F electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis and zeta potential were performed on a NanoZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). A Form1 + 3D printer (Formlabs, USA) was used to fabricate peripheral attachment of the SAFS. LC-2030 liquid chromatograph (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for real samples analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the SAFS and development of the SAFS-App
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 2 & Video S1, the SAFS consists of four main parts: 1) an adaptor (Fig. 2b) which can be easily tailored to be assembled onto smartphones of any brand via only slight mechanical modifications; 2) a spectrophotometer cradle (Fig. 2b & Fig. S1) for assembling all optical elements; 3) a holder with a black lid (Fig. 2b) for positioning cuvette; and 4) a Note 6 smartphone (Meizu, China) for collecting the change of both absorbance and fluorescence by using its rear-facing CMOS camera which equips a 16-megapixel backside-illuminated sensor (Fig. 2b, 4608×3456 pixels, ISO 800; white balance fixed; f-value 10; EV + 1). Detailed size information for manufacture of the SAFS is marked in Fig. 2b & Fig. S1. All the optical elements, including a white LED (Z3535RGBW-SH 3 W, Nation Star, China) as light source for absorption spectra, two ultraviolet LED (NVSU233B-D4 3 W, λ = 365 nm) as excitation light source for fluorescence spectra, plastic diffuser (PMMA, D10 mm), a plano- Convex Cylindrical Lenses (f = 7.7 mm, 9 mm × 7 mm), a plano-Convex Lenses (D6.2 mm, f = 7.2 mm), a diffraction grating (d = 1200 lines/mm, 15.4 mm × 15.4 mm × 4 mm) and a macro lens (D40 mm, f = 70 mm), were bought from local stores and assembled into the spectrophotometer cradle in a plug-and-play manner. The whole device was designed with SolidWorks software and fabricated with a Form1 + 3D printer using EP Epoxy resin as the printing material. The power supply circuit board was tightly fixed on the bottom of spectrometer cradle (Fig. 2a).

A user-friendly application, the SAFS-App, was developed using Java language to provide the end-user a stand-alone device. The most important function of the SAFS-App is to convert the spectral images captured by smartphone cameras to corresponding spectra. To this end, a three-color LED (Z3535RGBW-SH 3 W, Nation Star, China) with the center emission wavelengths at 473 nm, 520 nm and 625 nm was used to calibrate the wavelength-pixel relation which was subsequently used to determine the position of each wavelength in the spectral direction of images (Jian et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2014). In the non-spectral direction of images, an optimal area of pixels was selected as the region of interest (ROI), and the grayscale of ROI was calculated as the light intensity (Fig. S2). The other main functions of the SAFS-App include calibration curve plotting, result and testing location display, report generation and sharing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorometric detection of xanthine
ce:section.ce:para: AgNPs and CDs served as the colorimetric and fluorometric indicator, respectively. The CDs and AgNPs were prepared through a hydrothermal approach using citric acid and urea as co-reactants, and reduction of AgNO3 by citric acid, respectively. The synthesis protocols are described in detail elsewhere (Jiang, Jin, Sun, & Gui, 2019), and can also be found in Supporting Information.

A straightforward protocol containing four simple steps was established to detect xanthine with colorimetric and fluorometric dual-readouts: Step 1, prepare the indicator solution by simply mixing CDs (final concentration: 0.10 mg/mL) and AgNPs (final concentration: 0.12 mg/mL) in PBS buffer (0.015 mM, pH 7.4); Step 2, prepare 700 μL detection solution through simply mixing 1.0 mg/mL xanthine oxidase and xanthine standard solution with various concentrations (1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM) or samples containing unknown amount of xanthine, and allow the detection solution to be incubated at room temperature for 15 min; Step 3, mix 700 μL detection solution with 300 μL indicator solution, and allow the mixture to be incubated for another 15 min; Step 4, the mixture was transferred to a cuvette, and subsequently subjected to the SAFS, desktop UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer to collect absorption and fluorescence spectra.

To demonstrate the feasibility and practicability of the SAFS in real-world applications, grass carp (Ctenopharyngodon idella) and calf serum were selected as the representative real samples. Calf serum was bought from Merck, and grass carp was purchased from a local market. Calf serum was added with different amounts of xanthine to obtain spiked samples with the final xanthine concentrations of 0, 26.7, 66.7, and 102.02 μM. For fish sample, a grass carp was rapidly decapitated, and the resulting remains were incinerated after sampling. This process strictly followed the AVMA (American Veterinary Medical Association) guidelines for the euthanasia of animals: 2020 Edition. Fish was firstly ground into a homogenate with a grinder, then the fish homogenate was mixed with DI water at a ratio of 1:5 (w/w). After being filtered through a filter paper, the fish homogenate was mixed with different amounts of xanthine to obtain spiked samples with the final xanthine concentrations of 0, 3.21, 8.33, 16.53, and 26.08 μM.

It has been well-established that there is a certain correlation between the increase of xanthine content and the deterioration of fish (C. Li et al., 2019). Therefore, xanthine concentration in grass carp stored at 4 ℃ refrigerator for different times (10, 24, 48, 64, 80, 96, 112, and 128 h) was examined. Following the same preparation procedure as spiked fish samples, fish stored for different times was ground into a homogenate, mixed with DI water, filtered through a filter paper before determination.

For all the spiked samples and real samples, 700 μL sample solution was added with 1.0 mg/mL xanthine oxidase and incubated for 15 min. Then, after being added with 300 μL indicator solution and incubated for another 15 min, sample solutions were subjected to the SAFS for colorimetric and fluorometric detections. For validation, all the spiked samples and real samples were determined by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) in parallel following the standard protocol (Rong et al., 2015).

The assays were repeated at least three times to obtain consistent values. Results were presented as mean ± SD of the triplicate experiments. The statistical differences were analyzed by Bland-Altman analysis and paired Student’s t-test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the SAFS and development of the SAFS-App
ce:section.ce:para: The development of field-deployable spectrophotometers which can be operated by laypersons is accelerated by the dramatically increasing needs for quantitative POCT (JingJing Zhang, Lan, & Lu, 2020). In particular, there is an urgent need for portable spectrophotometers that can simultaneously collect absorption and fluorescence spectra in a reproducible and precise manner. With the help of smartphones’ CMOS sensors which are sensitive to light in the wavelength range of 300–1000 nm (Hussain & Bowden, 2021; Wang, Xue, Barron, & Chen, 2019) and gratings which can diffract incident light into light with different wavelengths along the spectral direction, design and cost-effective fabrication of portable “two-in-one” spectrophotometers can be substantially simplified (Fig. 2).

It has been well-established that capturing spectral images in a reproducible and high-quality manner is the basis of accurate and precise reconstruction of spectra. To this end, a spectrophotometer cradle was specifically designed to assemble all optical elements in correct alignment and eliminate the interference of ambient light, manufactured using 3D-printing technology, and sprayed with black paint to reduce specular reflection (Fig. 2b, d and f). The positions of all optical elements were preliminarily set up according to the calculation based on the optical parameters and simulation using professional software, and then verified by experiments. Namely, the model of the optical elements and their positioning along the optical path were meticulously modulated to accommodate a specific camera. If a camera with different imaging parameters were employed as the detector, either the model of the optical elements or their positioning along the optical path should be modulated to ensure precise collection of spectra. Specifically, under this consideration, all the optical elements are assembled into the cradle in a plug-and-play manner (Video S1), which facilitates adaption to different brands of smartphones and later maintenance. A holder with a black lid was designed to position a cuvette which was used to load samples. An adaptor was designed and 3D-printed to assemble the cradle onto smartphones which simultaneously served as controller, detector, monitor and analyzer of the SAFS. The weight and manufacturing cost of the SAFS were ∼230 g and ∼180.0 USD per set, respectively. This highlighted the SAFS’s advantageous features of portability and cost-effectiveness.

More importantly, the SAFS integrated two function units, namely, an absorption spectrum collection unit and a fluorescence spectrum collection unit. Fig. 2c and e illustrated the internal optical paths for absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum collection using the SAFS, respectively. The classic design of optical path used in most desktop absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometers was adopted for the SAFS with only slight modification. The light transmitted through the sample (under colorimetric detection mode, Fig. 2c) or the light emitted from the sample (under fluorometric detection mode, Fig. 2e) was firstly collimated by the plano-Convex Lenses, and then focused by the Cylindrical Lenses onto the transmission diffraction grating (1200 lines/mm). According to the diffraction grating equation, the diffraction grating was oriented at a 45° angle with respect to the light path. Finally, the spectral components split by the grating were captured simultaneously by the CMOS camera of smartphones through a macro lens to generate images with multicolored band. Same as desktop fluorescence spectrophotometers, fluorophores are excited at an orthogonal angle to the axis of light collection. According to the pixel resolution and the wavelength range of the camera, the resolution of the SAFS was calculated to be 0.319 nm/pixel in the spectral direction. In the non-spectral direction, the width of the colored band in the image was ∼180 pixels, suggesting that many independent CMOS sensors contributed to the spectral intensity of each wavelength. It is noteworthy that the SAFS can complete a spectral scanning in the 380–800 nm wavelength range in only 5 s, thanks to the ultrafast imaging speed of CMOS cameras.

The SAFS-App, a custom-designed Android App, was programmed to control the SAFS for measurements, convert the captured images to corresponding spectra, extract the light intensity of absorption peak or emission peak for subsequent quantitation, plot calibration curves, display results and testing locations, generate and share reports. The user-friendly interface of the SAFS-App together with the touch screen of smartphones made the SAFS easy to use. Fig. 2g showed the typical operation processing steps of plotting a calibration curve. Please refer to the Supporting Information for a concise operation manual and a demo vid. eo (Video S2) of the SAFS-App.

To precisely convert spectral images captured by the imaging system of a smartphone to corresponding spectra, the wavelength-pixel relation was calibrated by using a three-color LED at first. To this end, spectral images corresponding to 473 nm, 520 nm and 625 nm were captured using the SAFS, and converted to corresponding spectra using the SAFS-App. Then, linear fitting based on the least square method was used to determine the wavelength-pixel relation, because the peaks of corresponding spectra were known to be 473 nm, 520 nm and 625 nm, respectively. Finally, the position of each wavelength in the spectral direction of images could be determined according to the wavelength-pixel relation, and the light intensity of each wavelength could be extracted from ROI in the non-spectral direction of images according to the equation: grayscale value = (R + G + B)/3 (Fig. S2) (Wong, Liu, & Yu, 2014).

To validate the SAFS as a stand-alone portable spectrophotometer that can collect absorption and fluorescence spectra in a highly precise manner, spectrum collection consistency between the SAFS and laboratory counterparts was evaluated. To this end, blue-emissive CDs (λem = 445 nm), green-emissive coumarin 6 (λem = 525 nm) and yellow-emissive rhodamine 6G (λem = 560 nm) were subjected to the SAFS and Fluoromax-4P fluorescence spectrophotometer to collect their fluorescence spectra in parallel. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a and the upper row of Fig. 3b, only a slight wavelength shift of the emission peaks was observed for the same fluorophore. More importantly, well-resolved dual emissions at 445 nm and 560 nm for the mixture of blue-emissive CDs and yellow-emissive rhodamine 6G were clearly recorded by the SAFS. Similarly, the SAFS and DU730 UV/vis spectrophotometer recorded the absorption spectra with similar shapes but slightly shifted peaks for trypan blue (λmax = 590 nm), AgNPs (λmax = 420 nm), rhodamine 6G (λmax = 526 nm) (Fig. 3c and the lower row of Fig. 3b). In addition, absorption spectra of the mixture of trypan blue and AgNPs recorded by the SAFS and DU730 UV/vis spectrophotometer also exhibited the similar shape. Obviously, the SAFS installed with SAFS-App can perform the same functions as laboratory counterparts in an acceptable precision and accuracy, rendering the SAFS a convenient and flexible platform for on-site quantitation.

As depicted in Fig. 1, in this designed sensing system, xanthine oxidase serves as the recognition moiety for the specific detection of xanthine, while CDs function as the fluorometric indicator, and AgNPs serve a dual function as colorimetric indicator as well as fluorescence quencher of CDs. The indicator solution, namely, the CDs/AgNPs mixture solution, exhibits a canary-yellow color and weak fluorescence emission, because the inner filter effect (IFE) results in the fluorescence quench of CDs by AgNPs. However, H2O2, the product of xanthine under the specific catalysis of xanthine oxidase, can decompose AgNPs into Ag+ ions which exhibit no absorbance at all in the whole visible region.

Therefore, the presence of xanthine can specifically trigger the generation of H2O2, which subsequently produces the color change of the CDs/AgNPs mixture solution from canary-yellow to colorless and the restoration of blue fluorescence of CDs. Inspired by this phenomenon, an opportune method is designed for quantitation of xanthine using colorimetric and fluorometric dual-readouts. To realize quantitation at point-of-need by laypersons, both colorimetric and fluorometric signal changes of the indicator solution can be precisely recorded using the SAFS which is controlled by a smartphone installed with the user-friendly SAFS-App. Thanks to the powerful data-processing capabilities and convenient connectivity of smartphones, the quantitation results tagged with GPS coordinates of testing locations can be displayed on the screen and shared via wireless network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Blue-emissive CDs were designed as the fluorometric indicator, and facilely prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal approach using citric acid and urea as precursors. As revealed in Fig. 4 a, the CDs exhibited good water-solubility and average diameters of 3.5 nm. A lattice fringe spacing of 0.34 nm presented in HRTEM images revealed the high crystallinity of the CDs (Inset of Fig. 4a) (Wang, Li, Zhang, Wang, Zhang, & Huang, 2017; Xiao et al., 2020). XPS measurement revealed that the main components of the CDs were C, N and O (Fig. S4a), and there were abundant surface oxygen-containing groups on the as-prepared CDs (Fig. S4b – d), affording CDs excellent water solubility. In the meantime, AgNPs which exhibit strong and broad absorption in the visible region and can be rapidly etched by H2O2, were carefully designed as the colorimetric indicator, and facilely prepared via the reduction of AgNO3 by citric acid. TEM image revealed that AgNPs exhibited good water-solubility and an average diameter of 20 nm (Fig. 4b).

The as-prepared CDs could be excited in the wavelength range of 350–400 nm with an excitation peak at ∼380 nm (curve iii of Fig. 4c), and exhibited strong emission in the wavelength range of 400–600 nm with an emission peak at ∼450 nm (curve iv of Fig. 4c). As designed, AgNPs exhibited strong and broad absorption in the wavelength range of 350–550 nm with an absorption peak at ∼420 nm (curve ii of Fig. 4c). It is obvious that the absorption spectrum of AgNPs overlaps substantially with both the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of the as-prepared CDs, indicating the possible fluorescence quench of CDs by AgNPs via IFE mechanism. As anticipated, a gradual fluorescence quench of CDs was clearly observed, along with the increasing concentration of AgNPs (Fig. S3a). And the addition of AgNPs into the solution of CDs only caused negligible change on the fluorescence lifetime of CDs (Fig. S3b), authenticating the IFE-induced fluorescence quench of CDs (Zhao et al., 2016). In addition, upon the incubation with 200 μM H2O2 for 10 min, AgNPs with a diameter of ∼20 nm become only “nano dots” residue which are confirmed by EDS mapping to be Ag element (Fig. S3c – f). These results confirm the successful etching of AgNPs by H2O2 and also validate the mechanism of our proposed assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorometric detection of xanthine
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, sensing parameters of the CDs/AgNPs indicator solution for xanthine detection were optimized. As shown in Fig. S5a and b, the addition of 200 μM H2O2 into the CDs/AgNPs indicator solution resulted in the maximum fluorescence restoration at 450 nm (F450) was observed at the AgNPs-to-CDs ratio of 3, but the absorbance at 420 nm (A420) decreased along with the increasing AgNPs-to-CDs ratio. Considering the high extinction coefficient of AgNPs and the balance between colorimetric and fluorometric signals, the optimal AgNPs-to-CDs ratio was set to 2.5. Fig. S5c and d revealed that both fluorescence restoration and absorbance decrease of the CDs/AgNPs indicator solution in the presence of 200 μM H2O2 were changed dramatically for the first 10 min and stabled over the entire course of monitoring, suggesting the rapid etching of AgNPs by H2O2. It should take a certain amount time for xanthine oxidase to specifically catalyze the xanthine oxidation to generate enough H2O2 for AgNPs etching. Therefore, fluorescence restoration and absorbance decrease of the CDs/AgNPs indicator solution reached a plateau after 30 min reaction between xanthine oxidase and xanthine (Fig. S5e and f). Moreover, the evaluation on the effect of pH on the sensing system indicated the best pH condition lies within a neutral range for both colorimetric and fluorometric readout (Fig. S5g & Fig. 5 h).

As designed, H2O2 triggered a gradual increase of CDs fluorescence (F450) (Fig. S6a) and a gradual decrease of AgNPs absorbance (A420) (Fig. S6b). ΔF450 exhibited a linear range from 1.0 to 50.0 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.979 and a LOD of 0.35 μM (Fig. S6c), while ΔA420 exhibited a linear range from 2.0 to 50.0 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.979 and a LOD of 0.65 μM (Fig. S6d). Therefore, when using xanthine oxidase as the recognition moiety, the presence of xanthine induced the specific generation of H2O2, thus decreasing the absorbance of AgNPs and restoring the fluorescence of CDs. As anticipated, the quantitation of xanthine was also successfully realized using colorimetric and fluorometric readouts (Fig. 4d − g). ΔF450 exhibited a linear range from 1.0 to 100.0 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.987 and a LOD of 0.54 μM (Fig. 4g), while ΔA420 exhibited a linear range from 5.0 to 100.0 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.996 and a LOD of 1.65 μM (Fig. 4e). In comparison, this dual-readout xanthine assay featured with higher accuracy, better reliability and stronger flexibility, exhibited comparable detection sensitivity to that of previously reported assays or sensors for xanthine (Table S1).

Because of the high specificity of xanthine oxidase toward xanthine, this dual-readout xanthine assay is anticipated to exhibit a high specificity and anti-interference capacity for xanthine quantitation. Specificity and interference assays were performed by examining the effects of possible coexisting substances including inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+), and biologic species (adenine, l-ascorbic acid, uric acid, cysteine, glucose glycine and glutathione), on the colorimetric and fluorometric response of this xanthine sensing system. As anticipated, only negligible change in colorimetric (Fig. S4h) and fluorometric (Fig. 4i) response was observed for any examined substances, no matter in the absence or presence of xanthine. This result confirmed the good selectivity and anti-interference capability of this dual-readout xanthine assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site detection of xanthine in serum and fish using the SAFS
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the SAFS as a reliable device for colorimetric and fluorometric detection, a series of images of the CDs/AgNPs indicator solution upon reaction with detection solution containing xanthine oxidase and standard xanthine solutions of different concentrations (0, 1, 10, 20, 40, 60 µM), were captured by the SAFS using both colorimetric and fluorometric detection modes (Insets of Fig. 5a). Then, all the images were processed by the SAFS-App to generate calibration curves for xanthine quantitation (Fig. 5a). Performing this dual-readout assay using the SAFS, ΔF450 exhibited a linear range from 1.0 to 60.0 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.999 and a LOD of 0.38 μM, while ΔA420 exhibited a linear range from 1.0 to 60.0 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.984 and a LOD of 0.58 μM. Notably, compared with the assay performed using laboratory counterparts, a narrower linear range and a lower LOD were obtained using this stand-alone SAFS. However, to perform the assay using laboratory counterparts, the same sample should be subjected to a UV/vis spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer successively. Obviously, this significantly increases the operation complexity and the detection cost.

To validate the reliability of the SAFS, we directly compared the xanthine concentration determined by the SAFS, the desktop UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer on the same set of samples (N = 10) with unknown xanthine concentration ranging from 1 to 65 μM. As shown in Fig. 5b, two sets of xanthine concentrations determined using the colorimetric readout exhibited a good correlation (R2 = 0.985) with a slope of 1.037. Similarly, the analysis on results determined using fluorometric readout also revealed a satisfactory linear regression slope of 1.011 with a correlation coefficient of 0.991. These results confirmed the accuracy of the SAFS in xanthine quantitation was high and comparable with the laboratory counterparts, and the SAFS could do the work of two independent desktop instruments, namely, a UV/vis absorption spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The practicability of the SAFS as a stand-alone device was further validated by on-site quantitation of xanthine in fish and xanthine-free calf serum. Fish and calf serum samples spiked with different amounts of xanthine were quantitated by the SAFS-based sensing system and HPLC in parallel. As shown in Table 1 ., recoveries of the known spiked amounts of xanthine detected using the colorimetric readout by the SAFS were in the range of 88 % to 104 % with the RSD ranging from 0.73 % to 4.78 %. While, recoveries were found to be in the range of 92 % to 106 % with the RSD ranging from 1.46 % to 6.12 % when using the fluorometric readout. Of note, 3.21 μM xanthine spiked in serum samples is not detected using either our assay nor HPLC. It is believed that the complicated matrix of real samples causes this unsatisfactory result, because there is almost no sample pre-treatment before measurements. Various interfering factors in real samples, such as enzyme inhibitors, small bioactive molecules, and ions, might inevitably influence the detection outcomes to some extent. Anyway, the detection consistency between the SAFS and HPLC was confirmed by the Bland-Altman analysis as evidenced by the distribution of all scatter points within the consistency limit of 95 % (Fig. S7).

In addition, the concentrations of xanthine in fish after different storage time were quantitated using the SAFS-based sensing system and HPLC in parallel. The results were consistent with previous reports that the concentrations of xanthine in fish increased along with the storage time, even if the fish was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C (Fig. 5c & Table S2). These results indicated that xanthine can serve as a possible indicator of freshness long before any visible spoilages. Again, Bland-Altman analysis confirmed the detection consistency between HPLC and the SAFS, no matter the colorimetric readout or fluorometric readout (Fig. 5d). All the above results convincingly validated the practicability and reliability of the SAFS-based sensing system for point-of-need quantitation of xanthine in real samples by exhibiting a comparable accuracy to HPLC.

In conclusion, a smartphone-based “two-in-one” portable spectrophotometer, the SAFS, is developed and demonstrated to precisely collect absorption and fluorescence spectra in a reproducible and high-quality manner. Taking advantage of 3D printing technology, the SAFS with ingenious structures can be easily fabricated, providing high flexibilities in portability and versatility. By using xanthine oxidase as the recognition moiety, CDs as the fluorometric indicator and AgNPs as colorimetric indicator, a straightforward protocol containing only four simple steps is established and demonstrated for xanthine quantitation with the colorimetric and fluorometric dual readouts. The ease-of-use SAFS together with the straightforward sensing protocol enables accurate detection of xanthine in fish and serum anywhere, anytime, and by anyone. This smartphone-based sensing system highlights its remarkable features such as portability (∼230 g), accuracy (comparable to HPLC method), reliability (CV ≤5%) and cost-effectiveness (∼180.0 USD per set), enabling food safety inspection, home-health monitoring, and environment surveillance even by laypersons in low resource settings.

Ningxia Xu: Investigation, Data curation, Software. Meng Xiao: Supervision, Writing – original draft, Validation. Zipei Yu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Baohui Jin: Methodology, Investigation. Mengsu Yang: Supervision, Methodology, Validation. Changqing Yi: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China (JCYJ20210324140004013 and GJHZ20210705142200001), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFE0201800), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (no. 2023M730745) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137107.
